title: Jeera Aloo

subs.name: a pinch of chilli flakes or half a tsp of red chilli powder added at the tempering stage
a squeeze of lemon juice at the end gives the same tartness
ghee makes this richer and gives it a festival-food quality; it will no longer be vegan

steps.text: Boil the potatoes in salted water until a knife pierces them easily but they are not falling apart — about 15 minutes. Drain and cool slightly, then peel and cut into rough quarters.

Heat the oil in a kadai or non-stick frying pan over medium-high flame until shimmering. Add the cumin seeds and let them crackle for 10 seconds — they should turn darker brown and aromatic, not black and burnt.
Add the dried red chillies and asafoetida; stir for 5 seconds. Add turmeric and red chilli powder and stir once. Immediately tip in the boiled potato pieces and toss to coat in the spiced oil.
Cook the potatoes on medium-high flame for 3–4 minutes, turning occasionally, until they develop a light golden crust on some sides. Do not over-stir — let them sit so they can caramelise.
Add amchur and salt, toss once more to distribute, and take off the heat. Finish with a generous handful of fresh coriander and serve as a side dish with dal, roti or puri.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: amchur → a squeeze of lemon juice at the end gives the same tartness; dried-red-chilli → a pinch of chilli flakes or half a tsp of red chilli powder added at the tempering stage; oil → ghee makes this richer and gives it a festival-food quality; it will no longer be vegan
